edge: The task is considered fundamental in the context of audio signal processing.
Audio captioning is an example of a recognition scenario that relies on sound event detection.
Acoustic scene understanding is another application that uses sound event detection as a key component.
Existing SED methods are divided into frame-level and event-level approaches.
Frame-level and event-level approaches contrast in their approach to classifying and modeling sound events.

node_1: discriminative detr-based sound event detector transformer (sedt)
edge: Conventional SEDT uses a single decoding step with unmodified queries.

node_1: diffusion-infused generative detr-based sound event detector (diffsed)
edge: DiffSED employs a multi-step decoding process using noisy queries to account for correlation between frames.

node_2: robust representation of grounded event boundaries
edge: The diffusion-based decoder in DiffSED provides a more robust representation
DiffSED outperforms other end-to-end methods by a significant margin (6.62% absolute improvement for event-level and 4.03% for segment-level metrics).
DiffSED has a higher performance for both event-level and segment-level evaluation
The model improves audio-tagging results by 3.5% absolute
The model exploits the SED problem as generative learning within the denoising diffusion framework.
Comparison is made using ASF and SSAST
These are the backbone models used as competitors in the EPIC-Sounds experiment.
ASF is a competing model among pre-trained backbones.
SSAST is another competing model among pre-trained backbones.
DiffSED outperforms ASF with a significant improvement in Top-1 and Top-5 accuracies.
DiffSED also outperforms SSAST
All experiments use pre-trained ResNet-50 features for training and inference.
DiffSED's denoising strategy involves corrupting event latents in the continuous space.
DiffSED-BB uses discrete event proposals corrupted and projected as queries.
DiffSED-BB shows lesser performance compared to DiffSED
Both denoising variants perform best with 30 event proposals for audio tagging.
DiffSED-BB has lower scores in both event-level and segment-level metrics compared to DiffSED.
The work reformulates the SED problem from the generative learning perspective using the diffusion-based transformer framework.
This method involves adapting diffusion models with a focus on denoising noisy event latents for modeling global dependencies.
The design allows for efficient modeling while maintaining the ability to capture global relationships in sound events.
Both variants are evaluated for stability
The study measures the distribution of performance when evaluating each model instance with different random seeds to assess robustness.
Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the method compared to existing art on commonly used benchmark datasets.
The conclusion states that our method is superior to these alternatives.
